Hey, this is Russell Brunson and I'm excited for today's training. I'm gonna be teaching you guys how to use the star story solution script This is one that It's kind of funny the the background on how I kind of learned this when I first got into My online business. I remember I was learning all the different pieces and was so exciting for me And then someone was explaining copyrighting to me I remember just being so upset and not liking it and I was like I didn't want to learn I didn't want to do it. I didn't want to be part of it like I was so against copyrighting I thought it was a horrible thing and And then I tried to hire some copywriters and it was not cheap by any stretch of imagination I remember the first copywriter I hired was $10,000 which I saved up for like almost a year to hire her and And then show the copy and came back and it wasn't even that good I remember being really upset and so then just kind of default because I wanted to make money I had to learn how to write copy. I had a I was kind of forced into it because I couldn't keep affording this Yeah, I can afford to hire copywriters each time and all those things that kind of come with that So I was like fine. I just gotta learn this and so I started testing and trying things and and some and what was cool is It's actually interesting. I remember I was talking to Jeff Walker a while ago And he was one of the first to kind of that I heard him I heard someone explain this and it made a lot of sense to me. He was talking about how The biggest benefit of what you can do in your business is start publishing things start writing blog posts and emails and sending things out because when you do you see how people respond and what they respond to and things like that and And that's really the the key is Is is learning that so for me? That's kind of our control that wins one of our In the dot-com secrets lab testing book one of the split tests that we found for somebody else was added an add to cart button Above their video and increase their conversions by 38% So we did that and had a very similar result So for me, I've got an add to cart button above and below. And so it can work, but you're basically cramming something out someone's throat You know I talk a lot about how selling is kind of like the movie inception like we got a cause Inception side someone's mind where they they're taking on a journey and they're put into an environment in a selling situation Where it's their idea where they go through tonight? Wow, I really need this thing Okay, and that's kind of the that's kind of the goal here is that you're you're you're not really selling some as much as you're You're introducing them to somebody They can relate to you're showing the process in the story that person went through to find the solution that they're looking for And then you introduce a solution and you offer to him if you do that it changes the the dynamics kind of changes the whole perspective Okay, so again, that's kind of the game plan. I would just use Notepad I loved it. I don't like notepad in Mac very well, so I use I think it's called text mate But it's just like a text editor. So that's what I typically use some people I use Microsoft Word or Pages or whatever it doesn't really matter just whatever you want to type in I just like this because it strips out all the formatting I don't like having formatting when I'm writing copy the next thing to kind of know when you are when you do start writing Well, let me step back So first thing I do is I go through and I take all these things and no side right So pattern interrupt here core desire questions agitate past failures big promise intro star So I go through and I take this template right here and I break it down so I know where I'm going You've got a very clear path of the direction that I'm going with the sales letter Okay, and this will help you so much when you're doing it because because now you're following a path in a process, right? So that's that's kind of big things. I write that out the next thing is I start going through it I'm just gonna go and I'm try to write copy and these as fast as I can and one thing to understand when you're writing copy Is that there's there's two parts of your brain, right? there's left brain and there's right brain and And one side of your brain is very creative and can come up with with good copy The other side of your brain wants to edit things, right? it's all about like making sure structures right and that your punctuation right all sorts of weird things like that and The problem is that when you're writing copy if you're using both sides of your brain You'll never get this to happen because you write something creatively and then your brain starts editing and changing it And it kills the flow and so what you have to do is when you're doing this is just sit down and start writing And I know that the first few times you do this It'll be kind of a weird process, but you just write and you can't you can't edit like if you have a typo You do not stop you don't do punctuation. You just you just write crap as fast as you can Because that's gonna shut off your the analytical side of your brain That's gonna try to edit things because the analytical side will strangle your your copy It'll kill it as fast as you can as fast as it can okay So yeah turn that off and just write a lot of times I'll just close my eyes and write as fast as I can or sometimes What I'll do is I'll take out my iPhone and my iPhone there's a There's a recorder. I think it's I record I record or something like that and A lot of times open this this thing right here And I'll just click record on it and I'll just and I'll just start talking because a lot of times I talk I can turn off the analytical side of my brain. I can just tell stories and kind of fill things in I'll talk my way through it and then I'll send that that audio to someone on fiverr.com And get them transcribe and send it back to me A lot of times I'll take that and copy and paste those things into here and then from there I can use my analytical brain to edit them and make them sound better That way sometimes it's easier to talk copy than it is to to actually write copy because your your analytical brain Keep shutting you off. In fact, it's funny a lot of our high-end coaching students I'll do voxer coaching with them where it's kind of like a walkie-talkie and the last week They'll ask me you know, whatever and I'll like hey, here's copy. I'm like I'm gonna transcribe this and I'll click record And I'll start talking a lot of times easier for me to talk copy than to to write it So that's another kind of trick of the trade Let's do that. So those are kind of some of the things that I do. I hope that helps from some some actual application Application process, okay. There's it talks about how like, you know Every everything we do is patterns, right? You wake up in the morning you get in the shower when you when you get out of the shower How do you dry yourself? Okay, if you think next time about how you dry yourself with your towel every single day I guarantee you that you dry yourself the exact same way you start with your you know your head then your foot then your whatever the Thing is you the same thing when you drive the office you jump in your car and next you know your office like your brain slips into these patterns Where it's just is doing things right and so what happens is the big the big issue You have that you're competing against is it when you're sending out video cells or things like that people are sitting behind their desk They're in their comfort zone. They're in their pattern and and they've got this pattern the patterns not to buy Well, some people some people it is but for the most people their patterns not to go and just buy stuff, right? And so when you understand that you got to figure out they're in their pattern. They're in their comfort zone I've got to get them I got to figure out how to like how to break that pattern how to interrupt them to get them to pay attention to me Okay, because you got like literally seconds to get somebody I was reading a study the other day about how In the in the 80s the average attention span of a human was 20 minutes Okay, that's back when you know the mail matter show up once a day You might get one or two phone calls a day and you know that whatever it was but that attention span is 20 minutes Okay, and so you could write copy a lot differently back then than you can nowadays You know back then you could just You could you know have super long form cells there's gonna be that exciting because people have 20 minutes at a time They could focus on things anyway The study said that right now the average attention span of an American is seven seconds seven seconds That's less than a nap Okay, every seven seconds you're getting a text or a Facebook message or an email or something and you're you're losing this and so You've got to you've got to do something so good You're gonna grab this person by the shirt in the first like five seconds and like shake them and be like listen to me Because I got something cool to tell you okay, and if you don't have that pattern interrupt You'll watch your video sales letters and someone will start watching it in the first five seconds boom They're gone like it's it's amazing how short our attention spans are nowadays And so the pattern interrupt is one of the the keys probably one of the most Yes. I want my own business. Yes. So I'm agitating might their past failures And then from there I transitioned to the one big promise. Okay, I learned this from from Perry Belcher originally He told me that whenever they had a sales video or sales letter that would make two or three big promises it always Taint didn't work. He said that they had to only focus on one big promise that they had one big promise It would work. Okay, again, as soon as they introduced two or three it would bomb Okay, my problem Or my guess is that if you've done this stuff in the past most of your sales are probably promising a bunch of different things Okay, you can't you cannot do that. All right, so story starts here now. I learned this from Dagen Smith. He he became obsessed for like a couple years on storytelling and story selling and and Went through some intense stuff with it and then I remember he was talking to me So the one the coolest things he taught me was he said they all good stories They started a point of high drama, okay? So you don't go to movie and they start this movie and they start, you know So-and-so is born and then this happened this happened, you know 15 minutes and they're like and then this crazy thing happened No, because that's like your board you miss the whole point They're not gonna watch the whole movie, right? They start the movie with the crazy thing happening She's like whoa what just happened and then they boom they then they go back in time and they fill in the backstory They got them to that point of high drama, okay? so I always think like in the in the in the uh Story of the star. What's the point of high drama? For example one of my friends? He he used to weigh like almost 400 pounds I think he was a big dude and he told me that the when he when he decided to lose weight He was with his he's with his family at at an amusement park And he sat on one of the roller coasters and the guys could not connect the the thing It wouldn't snap shut and so they had lifted up and he had to get off And he had to do the walk of shame back and all these little kids were laughing and appointing his kids were super embarrassed Because he couldn't fit in the roller coaster thing Okay, now if I was selling a weight loss product that would be my point of high drama I think you know hey here's so-and-so. I've got a six pack. I'm awesome right now, but didn't always used to be this way in fact You know four years ago. I took my kids to the amusement park because we were really excited and when we were there They wanted to go on whatever the ride is the amazing roller coaster and Magnificent picture, but here's a picture of it And we were really excited and I got in and and it was out in the seat I tried to connect it and the thing wouldn't snap the guy kept pushing on pushing He could not get it to snap he said sorry sir You're too big to ride this ride and he lifted it up and I got off and as I was walking back. I remember I Remember looking over and at first I noticed the other kids pointing and laughing at me Which hurt but then I looked over and I saw my own kids and in their embarrassment of about me Was was a lot worse like it that that was that was something I couldn't I couldn't take it anymore I felt like somebody had kicked me in the gut and and I had so much pain that That I knew that I had to make well, I'm not gonna get I'm jumping ahead But anyway, you see it see how I do like I'm sharing that backstory and that's bringing them into an emotional painful part of my life Then then I can see that hopefully someone else is like, you know Either they've gone through something similar or they're scared They don't want to go through like wow that was really bad like like that's the path I'm on if I don't change right. I'm sharing this point of high drama Okay, and one thing if you notice when I tell stories is something else I learned from Dagan, you know a lot people tell stories like Yeah, so I got off the thing. I didn't fit. It was really embarrassing. Okay. That's like that's like a surface level and To draw a motion you've got to dig deeper. Okay, so instead of saying hey, I got off the thing and it didn't work You know, I got off and and I walked back and I couldn't ride the roller coaster and that sucked Yeah, I dig deeper like and then how did I feel and and I talk about my feelings like like I felt this like pit in my stomach they got bigger and and And it was painful and like and like the sweat in my hands was was beating up I could feel like my face getting red and flush You start trying to describe how you felt when you do that People feel what you're talking about. Okay? A big key that she has to copy is becoming good at stories and it's it's not just talking at a surface level It's digging deeper. It's not saying this is how I felt. It's actually describing how you felt Okay, describing the what your body felt like and how your hands felt in your head and your your you know Whatever those things are you got to explain those just like a good novelist, you know tells all those kind of things They don't just you know, like the I mean you look at some of these books are huge novels and 90% of it's just the the author Describing how the person's feeling and so you got to you got to start doing that when you're telling these stories because that's what's gonna suck somebody in okay, so So there's a point of high drama and then here I even open another look like you know and I was walking off I realized that it didn't always used to be this way, you know 23 years ago. I was in great shape I was an athlete. I did all sorts of things and da da da da da da da But then so you're telling the backstory to kind of fill up the point of high drama and this backstory somewhere in the back story Boom you hit a wall. Okay, and crap ton is a technical term This is where the money comes from. All right, and so like for example, I look at the weight loss industry, right? You got four food groups right here. Boom. I go back and add in images Add in video files things like that and then I will go and I'll just record a voiceover So I'll set my microphone and I'll just read this so I'd be like Yes, this is me and yes, I take all these supplements every day But before I tell you why let me ask you a question Do you want to launch a new company online? So I'll record this whole thing like that as an audio file and then I'll get all the PowerPoint slides And I just hire somebody for me I have my brother do it and he goes and he syncs all the audio in the video syncs it up and then from there It gives me back a video file that I can then plug into a click funnels and boom I've got my my sales video done.
